Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Identity

Discriminant

If

ax2+bx+c=0

then

Δ=b2−4ac

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Identity

Quadratic Formula

If

ax2+bx+c=0

then

x=2a−b±b2−4ac​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Explain why if ∣x∣≤c, c≥0 is true then x≤c is true as well .

If x is non-negative, then ∣x∣=x, so x≤c.

If x is negative, then ∣x∣=−x, so −x≤c which implies x≥−c. Since c≥0, we have −c≤c, so x≤c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Explain why if x≤c, c≥0 is true then ∣x∣≤c is not necessarily true as well .

Counterexample: Let x=−2, ∣x∣=2 and c=1. Then −2≤1 is true, but 2≤1.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Explain why −∣x∣≤x≤∣x∣.

If x is non-negative, then ∣x∣=x, so −∣x∣=−x≤x≤x=∣x∣.

If x is negative, then ∣x∣=−x, so −∣x∣=x≤x≤−x=∣x∣.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Solve ∣x∣≤c, c≥0.

−c≤x≤c x∈[−c,c]

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Solve ∣x∣≥c, c≥0.

x≤−c or x≥c x∈(−∞,−c]∪[c,∞)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Solve ∣x−a∣≤c, c≥0.

−c≤x−a≤c a−c≤x≤a+c x∈[a−c,a+c]

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Solve ∣x−a∣≥c, c≥0.

x−a≤−corx−a≥c x≤a−corx≥a+c x∈(−∞,a−c]∪[a+c,∞)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Solve x2≤c, c≥0.

x2x2​∣x∣​≤c≤c​≤c​​

The solution is

−c​≤x≤c​ x∈[−c​,c​]

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Solve x2≥c, c≥0.

x2x2​∣x∣​≥c≥c​≥c​​

The solution is

x≤−c​ or x≥c​ x∈(−∞,−c​]∪[c​,∞)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

True or false: From (a+b)2≥0 can we conclude that a+b≥0.

False. The correct conclusion is ∣x+y∣≥0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

True or false: If a≤b, then a2≤b2

False in general.

Counterexample: Let a=−3, b=2, then −3≤2 but 9≤4.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

True or false: If ∣a∣≤∣b∣ then a2≤b2.

True, because

∣a∣≤∣b∣⇒∣a∣2≤∣b∣2⇒a2≤b2

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

If a2≤b2, what inequality does this imply about ∣a∣ and ∣b∣?

∣a∣≤∣b∣

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

When solving an inequality a≤b, what happens with the inequality direction if we add a constant c to both sides?

Inequality direction stays the same.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

When solving an inequality a≤b, what happens with the inequality direction if we multiply both sides by a positive constant c>0?

Inequality direction stays the same.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

When solving an inequality a≤b, what happens with the inequality direction if we multiply both sides by a negative constant c<0?

Inequality direction flips.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

If a≤b and f is an increasing function, what happens to the inequality after applying f?

It stays the same:

f(a)≤f(b)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

If a≤b and f is a decreasing function, what happens to the inequality after applying f?

The direction flips:

f(a)≥f(b)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

If 0<a≤b, what inequality holds between 1/a and 1/b?

1/a≥1/b (reciprocal is decreasing for positive numbers).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Is it true that if a>b then ln(a)>ln(b)? Why?

Yes, if a>0 and b>0 then

a>b⟹ln(a)>ln(b)

because ln(x) is monotonic and strictly increasing.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Is it true that if a>b then ea>eb? Why?

Yes, for any a and b

a>b⟹ea>eb

because ex is monotonic and strictly increasing.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

True or false: If a≤b then a3≤b3.

True, because f(x)=x3 is strictly increasing on R.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove that ∣x∣≥0 for all real x.

By definition,

∣x∣={x,−x,​x≥0x<0​

In both cases the value is non-negative, hence

∣x∣≥0

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove the inequality ab≤∣a∣∣b∣.

  • Case 1: a,b≥0. Then ∣a∣=a, ∣b∣=b, so ab=∣a∣∣b∣.
  • Case 2: a,b≤0. Then ∣a∣=−a, ∣b∣=−b, so ab=(−a)(−b)=∣a∣∣b∣.
  • Case 3: a and b have opposite signs. Say a≥0, b≤0. Then ab≤0, but ∣a∣∣b∣≥0. So automatically ab≤∣a∣∣b∣. Same if a≤0, b≥0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

What is the triangle inequality?

The triangle inequality states that for any real numbers a and b:

∣a+b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove the triangle inequality ∣a+b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣.

Let's square both sides:

∣a+b∣2≤(∣a∣+∣b∣)2

We know that

∣a+b∣2∣a∣2∣b∣2​=(a+b)2=a2=b2​

so

a2+2ab+b2ab​≤a2+2∣a∣∣b∣+b2≤∣a∣∣b∣​

The last inequality is true for any a and b, so the triangle inequality is true as well.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

What is the reverse triangle inequality?

The reverse triangle inequality states that for any real numbers a and b:

∣∣a∣−∣b∣∣≤∣a−b∣

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove the reverse triangle inequality.

Apply triangle inequality to a=(a−b)+b:

∣a∣=∣(a−b)+b∣≤∣a−b∣+∣b∣

Rearranging:

∣a∣−∣b∣≤∣a−b∣

Swap a,b to get:

∣b∣−∣a∣≤∣a−b∣

Combining both gives:

​∣a∣−∣b∣​≤∣a−b∣

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

What is the AM–GM inequality?

The AM–GM inequality states that for for any real number a,b≥0:

2a+b​≥ab​

Equality holds iff a=b.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove the AM-GM inequality.

(a−b)2a2−2ab+b2a2−2ab+4ab+b2a2+2ab+b2(a+b)22a+b​​≥0≥0≥4ab≥4ab≥4ab≥ab​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

If 0≤a≤b, what can we say about an and bn for integer n≥1?

0≤an≤bn

Powers preserve order for non-negative numbers.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove that x2+1≥2x for all real x.

Rearrange:

x2−2x+1≥0 (x−1)2≥0

which is always true.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove that a2+b2≥2ab.

a2+b2−2ab=(a−b)2≥0

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Inequality

Prove that (a−b)2≥0 implies a2+b2≥2ab.

Expand:

(a−b)2=a2−2ab+b2≥0

Rearrange:

a2+b2≥2ab

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Identity

sinα⋅sinβ
21​[cos(α−β)−cos(α+β)]

Related

sinα⋅sinβcosα⋅cosβsinα⋅cosβcosα⋅sinβ​=21​[cos(α−β)−cos(α+β)]=21​[cos(α−β)+cos(α+β)]=21​[sin(α+β)+sin(α−β)]=21​[sin(α+β)−sin(α−β)]​

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 2::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Identity

21​[cos(α−β)−cos(α+β)]
sinα⋅sinβ

Related

sinα⋅sinβcosα⋅cosβsinα⋅cosβcosα⋅sinβ​=21​[cos(α−β)−cos(α+β)]=21​[cos(α−β)+cos(α+β)]=21​[sin(α+β)+sin(α−β)]=21​[sin(α+β)−sin(α−β)]​

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Identity

sinhx=…
sinhx=2ex−e−x​

coshx=…
coshx=2ex+e−x​

tanhx=…
tanhx=ex+e−xex−e−x​

ex= in terms of sinh(x) and cosh(x)
ex=coshx+sinhx

e−x=coshx−sinhx

sinx=…

Express as a hyperbolic function

sinx=−isinh(ix)=2ieix−e−ix​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

sinhx=…

Express as a trigonometric function

sinhx=−isin(ix)=2ex−e−x​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

cosx=…

Express as a hyperbolic function

cosx=cosh(ix)=2eix+e−ix​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

coshx=…

Express as a trigonometric function

coshx=cos(ix)=2ex+e−x​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

cosh2x+sinh2x=…
cosh2x+sinh2x=cosh(2x)

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

1+sinh2x=…
1+sinh2x=cosh2x

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

1−tanh2x=…
1−tanh2x=sech2x

arcsinh(x)=…
arcsinh(x)=ln(x+x2+1​)

arccosh(x)=…

arctanh(x)=…
arctanh(x)=21​ln(1−x1+x​),∣x∣<1

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

arccoth(x)=…
arccoth(x)=21​ln(x−1x+1​),∣x∣>1

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Euler's Formula

eixe−ix​=cosx+isinx=cosx−isinx​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

De Moivre's Formula

(cos(x)+isin(x))n(cosh(x)+isinh(x))n​=cos(nx)+isin(nx)=cosh(nx)+isinh(nx)​

e=n→∞lim​(1+n1​)n

ex=n→∞lim​(1+nx​)n

e=n→0lim​(1+n)1/n

ex=n→0lim​(1+n)x/n

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of 1−x1​

For ∣x∣<1:

1−x1​​=1+x+x2+x3+⋯=n=0∑∞​xn​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of 1+x1​

For ∣x∣<1:

1+x1​​=1−x+x2−x3+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−x)n​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ex

For any x∈R:

ex​=1+x+2!x2​+3!x3​+⋯=n=0∑∞​n!xn​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of e−x

For any x∈R:

e−x​=1−x+2!x2​−3!x3​+⋯=n=0∑∞​n!(−x)n​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ln(x)

ln(x)​=(x−1)−2(x−1)2​+3(x−1)3​−4(x−1)4​+⋯=n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1n(x−1)n​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ln(1−x)

ln(1−x)​=−x−2x2​−3x3​−4x4​−⋯=−n=1∑∞​nxn​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ln(1+x)

ln(1+x)​=x−2x2​+3x3​−4x4​+⋯=n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1nxn​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Trigonometric Identity

Taylor series expansion of sin(x)

For any x∈R:

sin(x)​=x−3!x3​+5!x5​−7!x7​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−1)n(2n+1)!x2n+1​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Trigonometric Identity

Taylor series expansion of cos(x)

For any x∈R:

cos(x)​=1−2!x2​+4!x4​−6!x6​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−1)n(2n)!x2n​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Trigonometric Identity

Taylor series expansion of arctan(x)

arctan(x)​=x−3x3​+5x5​−7x7​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−1)n2n+1x2n+1​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Hyperbolic Identity

Taylor series expansion of sinh(x)

sinh(x)​=x+3!x3​+5!x5​+7!x7​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(2n+1)!x2n+1​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Hyperbolic Identity

Taylor series expansion of cosh(x)

cosh(x)​=1+2!x2​+4!x4​+6!x6​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(2n)!x2n​​

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Laplace Transforms

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Laplace-Transform

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 2::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Laplace Transforms

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Laplace-Transform

s2F(s)−sf(0)−f′(0)
L{f′′(t)}

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Which indeterminate quotient forms can L'Hopital's Rule handle directly?

L'Hopital's Rule applies directly to quotients with form

00​or±∞±∞​.

It does not apply directly to 0⋅∞, ∞−∞, 1∞, 00, or ∞0; those must first be rewritten.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

What differentiability assumptions are needed before applying L'Hopital's Rule near x=a?

The functions f and g must be differentiable on a punctured neighborhood of a. The value at a itself need not be defined.

A punctured neighborhood means values sufficiently close to a, excluding a itself.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Why does L'Hopital's Rule require g′(x)=0 near the limiting point?

The quotient g′(x)f′(x)​ must be defined near the limiting point. The proof also uses a mean-value-theorem comparison between increments of f and g, which requires the denominator derivative not to vanish on the interval being compared.

Extra

The condition is about g′, not about g at the limiting point. In a 00​ case, g(x)→0 is expected.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

What must be true of limx→a​g′(x)f′(x)​ before L'Hopital's Rule gives a conclusion?

The derivative quotient limit must exist as a finite number or diverge to +∞ or −∞. If this limit does not exist, L'Hopital's Rule gives no conclusion about limx→a​g(x)f(x)​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Does L'Hopital's Rule require f(a) and g(a) to be defined?

No. The rule concerns values of f and g near a, not necessarily at a. It is enough that the relevant limits and differentiability hypotheses hold on a punctured neighborhood of a.

Extra

Changing f(a) or g(a), when those values are defined, does not change limx→a​g(x)f(x)​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Why is the form 00​ or ∞∞​ essential in L'Hopital's Rule?

Because the rule compares the relative rates at which the numerator and denominator approach their limiting behavior. If the quotient is not indeterminate, ordinary limit laws may already determine the limit, and differentiating numerator and denominator can change the answer.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Construct a simple counterexample showing that L'Hopital's Rule cannot be used on a non-indeterminate quotient.

As x→0,

x→0lim​1+xx​=0,x→0lim​(1+x)′(x)′​=1.

The original quotient is not 00​ or ∞∞​, so L'Hopital's Rule is not applicable.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

What checks are still required in an ∞∞​ problem before applying L'Hopital's Rule?

One must check that f and g are differentiable near the limiting point, g′(x)=0 there, the quotient really has form ±∞±∞​, and the derivative quotient limit exists as a finite number or ±∞.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Give an example where the original quotient limit exists but the derivative quotient limit does not.

For x→∞,

xx+sinx​→1,x′(x+sinx)′​=1+cosx

has no limit. So existence of the derivative quotient limit is sufficient for L'Hopital's Rule, not necessary for the original limit to exist.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

How can a product limit of the form 0⋅∞ be rewritten for possible use of L'Hopital's Rule?

Rewrite the product as a quotient. If f(x)→0 and h(x)→∞, then one possible rewrite is

f(x)h(x)=1/h(x)f(x)​.

This may produce a 00​ form.

Extra

Another possible rewrite is h(x)/(1/f(x)), which may produce an ∞∞​ form.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

How can a difference limit of the form ∞−∞ be prepared for possible use of L'Hopital's Rule?

Rewrite the difference into a quotient, often by combining terms over a common denominator, rationalizing, or factoring. L'Hopital's Rule can be considered only after the rewritten expression has form 00​ or ∞∞​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

How can a limit of f(x)g(x) be transformed before using L'Hopital's Rule?

Set

y=f(x)g(x)

and take logarithms:

lny=g(x)ln(f(x)).

Then analyze the limit of g(x)ln(f(x)), usually after rewriting it as a quotient.

Extra

This requires f(x)>0 near the limiting point if real logarithms are being used.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

After finding limlny=A for y=f(x)g(x), what limit can be concluded?

If the logarithm setup is valid and lny→A, then

y→eA.

Thus

limf(x)g(x)=eA.

Extra

This conclusion uses continuity of the exponential function.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

What happens when L'Hopital's Rule is applied one-sidedly to limx→0​x2sinx​?

The derivative quotient is

(x2)′(sinx)′​=2xcosx​.

As x→0+, this tends to +∞. As x→0−, this tends to −∞.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Why does limx→0​x2sinx​ fail to exist as a two-sided limit?

The right-hand limit is +∞, while the left-hand limit is −∞. Since the one-sided limits do not agree, the two-sided limit does not exist.

Extra

L'Hopital's Rule can support the one-sided conclusions here because each side satisfies the appropriate hypotheses.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

What must be true before applying L'Hopital's Rule a second time?

The new quotient after the first differentiation must again have form 00​ or ±∞±∞​, satisfy the differentiability and nonzero-denominator-derivative hypotheses, and have a derivative quotient limit to evaluate.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Why is repeated L'Hopital use not just "differentiate until the expression looks simple"?

Each use of the rule has its own hypotheses. If a later quotient is no longer indeterminate, or if the new derivative quotient is undefined or has no limit, the next application is not justified.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

What theorem underlies the proof of L'Hopital's Rule in the 00​ case?

Cauchy's Mean Value Theorem. It relates the quotient of increments

g(x)−g(a)f(x)−f(a)​

to a derivative quotient

g′(c)f′(c)​

for some c between a and x.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory LHopitals-Rule

Give the proof idea for L'Hopital's Rule in the 00​ case after applying Cauchy's Mean Value Theorem.

After redefining f(a)=g(a)=0, Cauchy's Mean Value Theorem gives

g(x)f(x)​=g′(c)f′(c)​

for some c between a and x. As x→a, also c→a, so if g′f′​→L, then gf​→L.

Extra

This is a proof sketch; endpoint and one-sided details are being compressed.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What is a continuous function?

A function f(x) is continuous at a point x=c if:

  • f(c) exists: The function is defined at the point c.
  • limx→c​f(x) exists: The limit of the function as x approaches c from both the left and right sides is the same.
  • limx→c​f(x)=f(c): The limit of the function as x approaches c is equal to the actual value of the function at c.

If a function is continuous at every point in its domain, it is called a continuous function.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What do the one-sided limits limx→c−​f(x) and limx→c+​f(x) mean, and how do they relate to the two-sided limit limx→c​f(x)?

limx→c−​f(x) is the value f(x) approaches as x approaches c only through values less than c; limx→c+​f(x) is the analogous limit through values greater than c.

The two-sided limit limx→c​f(x) exists (as a finite number) if and only if both one-sided limits exist, are finite, and are equal to each other; its value is then their common value.

Extra

This equivalence is why one-sided limits are the natural tool for classifying discontinuities: whether they exist, are finite, and agree determines which kind of discontinuity occurs at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What makes a discontinuity at x=c removable?

Both one-sided limits exist, are finite, and agree:

x→c−lim​f(x)=x→c+lim​f(x)=L,

but either f(c) is undefined or f(c)=L.

It is called removable because defining (or redefining) f(c)=L makes f continuous at c, without changing f anywhere else.

Extra

Example: f(x)=x−1x2−1​ at x=1; the limit is 2, but f(1) is undefined.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What makes a discontinuity at x=c a jump discontinuity?

Both one-sided limits exist and are finite, but they disagree:

x→c−lim​f(x)=x→c+lim​f(x).

Extra

Unlike a removable discontinuity, no single choice of f(c) can make f continuous at c — the left and right values genuinely disagree.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Why are removable and jump discontinuities both classified as discontinuities of the first kind?

Both share the requirement that limx→c−​f(x) and limx→c+​f(x) each exist and are finite. They differ only in whether the two one-sided limits agree:

  • equal one-sided limits, but f(c) undefined or wrong → removable
  • unequal one-sided limits → jump

Related

Contrast with discontinuities of the second kind, where at least one one-sided limit fails to exist as a finite number.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What characterizes a discontinuity of the second kind (an essential discontinuity)?

At least one of limx→c−​f(x) or limx→c+​f(x) fails to exist as a finite number — either it does not exist at all, or it is infinite.

Extra

This is the complement of the first-kind case: first kind requires both one-sided limits to exist and be finite, so any failure of that condition puts the discontinuity in the second kind.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Why is the discontinuity of f(x)=1/x at x=0 essential rather than removable?

Because limx→0−​f(x)=−∞ and limx→0+​f(x)=+∞: neither one-sided limit is a finite number, so no value assigned to f(0) could make f continuous there.

Extra

This case is sometimes singled out as an infinite discontinuity, a special case of an essential discontinuity where the one-sided limit(s) are infinite rather than simply nonexistent.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Give an example of an essential discontinuity caused by oscillation rather than by an infinite limit, and explain why.

f(x)=sin(1/x) at x=0: as x→0, 1/x grows without bound, so sin(1/x) oscillates through every value in [−1,1] infinitely often. Neither one-sided limit exists, since f(x) never settles near a single value.

Extra

This is called an oscillating discontinuity, a subtype of essential discontinuity.

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Notation for the left-hand limit of f at c.

x→c−lim​f(x)

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 2::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

x→c−lim​f(x)

Notation for the left-hand limit of f at c.

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Notation for the right-hand limit of f at c.

x→c+lim​f(x)

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 2::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

x→c+lim​f(x)

Notation for the right-hand limit of f at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What do the shorthand notations f(c−) and f(c+) mean, as used when discussing discontinuities?

They abbreviate the one-sided limits:

f(c−)f(c+)​:=x→c−lim​f(x):=x→c+lim​f(x)​

Extra

This is common shorthand in analysis. It is not evaluating f at some point called "c−" — c− is not itself an argument of f.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Why does differentiability at c imply continuity at c?

For x=c near c,

f(x)−f(c)=x−cf(x)−f(c)​⋅(x−c).

If f′(c) exists, taking x→c gives

x→clim​(f(x)−f(c))=f′(c)⋅0=0,

so limx→c​f(x)=f(c), which is exactly continuity at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Construct a counterexample showing continuity at a point does not imply differentiability there.

f(x)=∣x∣ at c=0: it is continuous since limx→0​∣x∣=0=f(0). But

h→0−lim​h∣h∣−0​=−1,h→0+lim​h∣h∣−0​=1,

so the one-sided derivatives disagree and f′(0) does not exist.

Related

The disagreement here is exactly analogous to a jump discontinuity in the one-sided limits of f itself — here it is the one-sided limits of the difference quotient that disagree instead.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What does it mean for f to be continuous from the right (resp. from the left) at c?

f is continuous from the right at c if f(c) exists and limx→c+​f(x)=f(c). Continuous from the left is the same statement with limx→c−​f(x)=f(c).

Extra

Two-sided continuity at c is equivalent to being continuous from both the left and the right at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Why is continuity of f on a closed interval [a,b] stated using one-sided continuity at the endpoints a and b?

Outside [a,b], f is not defined, so a genuine two-sided limit at a or b cannot be formed. All that can be asked is that f behave well from the side that lies in the domain: continuous from the right at a, and continuous from the left at b.

Extra

This is precisely the hypothesis theorems like the IVT and EVT need at the endpoints of [a,b].

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What condition must hold at a boundary point c for a piecewise-defined function to be continuous there?

The left-hand limit (computed from the piece defined for x<c), the right-hand limit (from the piece for x>c), and f(c) itself must all agree:

x→c−lim​f(x)=x→c+lim​f(x)=f(c).

Extra

It is not enough for the two pieces' formulas to agree in value at c if one of them is not itself continuous up to the boundary — the limiting behavior, not just the plugged-in value, is what must match.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

State the ε-δ definition of continuity of f at c.

f is continuous at c if for every ε>0 there exists δ>0 such that for all x in the domain of f,

∣x−c∣<δ⟹∣f(x)−f(c)∣<ε.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

How does the ε-δ definition of continuity relate to the limit-based definition (f(c) exists, limx→c​f(x) exists, and equals f(c))?

They are the same statement at different levels of precision. The limit-based definition names the ingredients: f(c) must exist, the limit must exist, and the two must agree. The ε-δ definition is what "limx→c​f(x)=f(c)" precisely means: however small a tolerance ε is demanded on the output, some tolerance δ on the input suffices to guarantee it.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Construct a counterexample showing the IVT's conclusion can fail if f is not continuous on [a,b].

Let f(x)=−1 for x∈[a,m) and f(x)=1 for x∈[m,b], for some m∈(a,b). Then f(a)=−1 and f(b)=1, so 0 lies between them, but f never takes the value 0 anywhere on [a,b]. The jump discontinuity at m lets f skip over 0 entirely.

Related

Compare with the Intermediate Value Theorem card in 03-differentiation.note, which requires continuity on [a,b] as a hypothesis.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Construct a counterexample showing the EVT's conclusion can fail if f is not continuous on [a,b].

Let f(x)=x for x∈[0,1) and f(1)=0, on the closed bounded interval [0,1]. Values of f(x) get arbitrarily close to 1 as x→1−, but f never equals 1 since f(1)=0; so f has no maximum on [0,1], even though the interval is closed and bounded.

Related

Compare with the Extreme Value Theorem, which requires continuity on [a,b] as a hypothesis.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

Define the Dirichlet function and explain why it is discontinuous at every real number.

D(x)={10​x∈Qx∈/Q​

Both the rationals and the irrationals are dense in R, so every neighborhood of any point c contains points where D=1 and points where D=0. Hence limx→c​D(x) does not exist at any c, and D is discontinuous everywhere.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What is significant about the Weierstrass function, and why is it beyond the reach of elementary calculus to fully justify?

The Weierstrass function is continuous at every real number but differentiable at none of them — a classical theorem, stated here without proof. It shows that continuity, even everywhere, does not imply any local smoothness: unlike ∣x∣, which fails to be differentiable at just one point, this function has no well-defined tangent direction anywhere.

Extra

Its standard proof relies on tools beyond elementary calculus, such as uniform convergence of an infinite series of cosine terms.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

What is a critical point?

A critical point of a function f(x) is a point in the domain of f where either:

  • The derivative f′(x) is zero, or
  • The derivative f′(x) does not exist.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

What is the main idea of the first derivative test?

The first derivative test classifies a critical point by checking whether f′ changes sign on the left and right of that point.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

What hypotheses are needed to use the first derivative test at a critical point c?

The function f should be continuous near c and differentiable on each side of c, though f′(c) may be zero or undefined.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

In the first derivative test, what does f′ changing from positive to negative at c imply?

It implies that f has a local maximum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

In the first derivative test, what does f′ changing from negative to positive at c imply?

It implies that f has a local minimum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

In the first derivative test, what does it mean if f′ is positive on both sides of c?

The function is increasing through c, so f has no local extremum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

In the first derivative test, what does it mean if f′ is negative on both sides of c?

The function is decreasing through c, so f has no local extremum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Why does f′ changing from positive to negative indicate a local maximum?

Positive f′ means f is increasing before c, and negative f′ means f is decreasing after c. So nearby values rise up to f(c) and then fall away from it.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Why does f′ changing from negative to positive indicate a local minimum?

Negative f′ means f is decreasing before c, and positive f′ means f is increasing after c. So nearby values fall down to f(c) and then rise away from it.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Give an example of a stationary critical point that is not a local extremum.

f(x)=x3 has f′(0)=0, but f′(x)=3x2>0 for x=0. The function is increasing through 0, so 0 is not a local extremum.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

What is the main idea of the second derivative test?

The second derivative test classifies a stationary critical point c as a local maximum, a local minimum, or neither using the sign of f′′(c).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

What hypotheses are needed to use the second derivative test at c?

One needs f′(c)=0 and f′′(c) to exist.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

In the second derivative test, what does f′′(c)>0 imply when f′(c)=0?

It implies that f has a local minimum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

In the second derivative test, what does f′′(c)<0 imply when f′(c)=0?

It implies that f has a local maximum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

In the second derivative test, what does f′′(c)=0 imply?

The test is inconclusive; c may be a local minimum, a local maximum, or neither.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Why does f′′(c)>0, together with f′(c)=0, indicate a local minimum?

Near c, f′ changes from negative to positive. By the first derivative test, that gives a local minimum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Why does f′′(c)<0, together with f′(c)=0, indicate a local maximum?

Near c, f′ changes from positive to negative. By the first derivative test, that gives a local maximum at c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Give an example where f′′(c)=0 but c is a local minimum.

f(x)=x4 at c=0. Here f′(0)=0, f′′(0)=0, and f(x)≥f(0) near 0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Give an example where f′′(c)=0 but c is a local maximum.

f(x)=−x4 at c=0. Here f′(0)=0, f′′(0)=0, and f(x)≤f(0) near 0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Give an example where f′′(c)=0 and c is not a local extremum.

f(x)=x3 at c=0. Here f′(0)=0, f′′(0)=0, but the function is increasing through 0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Why can the first derivative test classify some points where the second derivative test cannot?

The first derivative test uses sign changes of f′ around c, while the second derivative test uses only f′′(c). If f′′(c)=0 or does not exist, the first derivative test may still work.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Why do derivative tests classify local extrema rather than absolute extrema by themselves?

They only describe behavior near a critical point. Absolute extrema require comparing values across the whole domain or interval, including endpoints when relevant.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Give a geometric interpretation of a function being concave up on an interval.

On that interval, the graph lies above each of its tangent lines (equivalently, each tangent line lies below the graph).

Extra

Concave down is the mirror image: the graph lies below each of its tangent lines.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

How is concavity related to the monotonicity of f′?

f is concave up on an interval I exactly when f′ is increasing on I, and concave down exactly when f′ is decreasing on I.

Extra

This is usually taken as the working definition of concavity; the tangent-line picture is the geometric interpretation of the same fact.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

For a twice-differentiable f, what does f′′(x)>0 on an interval I imply about concavity?

f is concave up on I, since f′′>0 means f′ is strictly increasing on I.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

For a twice-differentiable f, what does f′′(x)<0 on an interval I imply about concavity?

f is concave down on I, since f′′<0 means f′ is strictly decreasing on I.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

What is an inflection point of f?

A point c where f is continuous and the concavity of f changes — from concave up to concave down, or vice versa — as x passes through c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

If f′′(c) exists and c is an inflection point, what must be true of f′′(c), and why?

f′′(c)=0. Since concavity switches at c, f′ has a local extremum at c; if (f′)′(c)=f′′(c) exists, Fermat's theorem applied to f′ forces f′′(c)=0.

Extra

Necessary, not sufficient — f′′(c)=0 alone does not guarantee c is an inflection point.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Give a counterexample showing f′′(c)=0 does not imply c is an inflection point.

f(x)=x4,f′′(x)=12x2.

Then f′′(0)=0, but f′′(x)≥0 everywhere, so f is concave up on all of R and the concavity never changes at 0.

Related

The same function already shows f′′(c)=0 does not determine whether c is a local extremum.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Verify that f(x)=x3 has an inflection point at x=0.

f′′(x)=6x,

negative for x<0 and positive for x>0. So f is concave down on (−∞,0) and concave up on (0,∞); concavity changes at 0, so 0 is an inflection point.

Related

The same function already appears as a stationary critical point that is not a local extremum.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

How does concavity at a critical point relate to the second derivative test?

If f′(c)=0 and f is concave up near c (f′′(c)>0), then c is a local minimum; if f is concave down near c (f′′(c)<0), c is a local maximum. The second derivative test is exactly this concavity criterion applied at a stationary point.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the Extreme Value Theorem (EVT).

The Extreme Value Theorem states that if a real-valued function f is continuous on the closed and bounded interval [a,b], then f must attain a maximum and a minimum, each at least once.

That is, there exist numbers c and d in [a,b] such that: f(c)≤f(x)≤f(d)∀x∈[a,b].

In simpler terms, a continuous function on a closed interval will always have a highest point and a lowest point.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the Rolle's Theorem.

The Rolle's Theorem is a special case of the Mean Value Theorem.

The Rolle's Theorem states that if a function f(x) is:

  • Continuous on a closed interval [a,b]
  • Differentiable on the open interval (a,b)
  • f(a)=f(b) (the function has the same value at the endpoints)

Then there exists at least one point c in the open interval (a,b) such that f′(c)=0.

Geometrically, this means if a continuous and differentiable curve starts and ends at the same height, there must be at least one point in between where the tangent line is horizontal (i.e., the slope is zero).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the Mean Value Theorem (MVT).

The Mean Value Theorem is a generalization of Rolle's Theorem.

The Mean Value Theorem states that if a function f(x) is:

  • Continuous on a closed interval [a,b]
  • Differentiable on the open interval (a,b)

Then there exists at least one point c in the open interval (a,b) such that the instantaneous rate of change (the derivative f′(c)) is equal to the average rate of change over the interval.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

f′(c)=b−af(b)−f(a)​

Or, alternatively:

f(c)=b−a1​∫ab​f(x)dx

The Rolle's Theorem can be derived from the Mean Value Theorem: if f(a)=f(b), then the right side of the Mean Value Theorem equation becomes

f′(c)=b−af(a)−f(a)​=b−a0​

which means f′(c)=0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT).

The Intermediate Value Theorem states that if a function f(x) is continuous on a closed interval [a,b], and L is any value between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one number c in the interval [a,b] such that f(c)=L.

Intuitively, if you can draw the graph of a function without lifting your pencil, and it goes from one y-value to another, it must pass through all the y-values in between.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Prove that dydx​⋅dxdy​=1

yxf−1(f(x))​=f(x)=f−1(y)=x​

Differentiate both sides with respect to x using the chain rule:

dx/dy(f−1)′(f(x))​​⋅dy/dxf′(x)​​=1

which is the same as

dydx​⋅dxdy​=1

because

(f−1)′(f(x))=(f−1)′(y)=dydx​

and

f′(x)=dxdy​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Derive dxd​ln(x)

yx​=ln(x)=ey​

Differentiate both sides implicitly with respect to x using the chain rule:

dxd​[x]1dxdy​​=dxd​[ey]=ey⋅dxdy​=ey1​​

Express ey in terms of x:

ey​=elnx=x​

Substitute back:

dxdy​=x1​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Derive dxd​arcsin(x)

yx​=arcsin(x)=sin(y)​

Differentiate both sides implicitly with respect to x using the chain rule:

dxd​[x]1dxdy​​=dxd​[sin(y)]=cos(y)⋅dxdy​=cos(y)1​​

Express cos(y) in terms of x:

cos(y)​=1−sin2(y)​=1−x2​​

Substitute back:

dxdy​=1−x2​1​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Derive dxd​arccos(x)

yx​=arccos(x)=cos(y)​

Differentiate both sides implicitly with respect to x using the chain rule:

dxd​[x]1dxdy​​=dxd​[cos(y)]=−sin(y)⋅dxdy​=−sin(y)1​​

Express sin(y) in terms of x:

sin(y)​=1−cos2(y)​=1−x2​​

Substitute back:

dxdy​=−1−x2​1​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Derive dxd​arctan(x)

yx​=arctan(x)=tan(y)​

Differentiate both sides implicitly with respect to x using the chain rule:

dxd​[x]1dxdy​​=dxd​[tan(y)]=sec2(y)⋅dxdy​=sec2(y)1​​

Express sec2(y) in terms of x:

sec2(y)​=1+tan2(y)=1+x2​

Substitute back:

dxdy​=1+x21​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

Derive ∫f−1(y)dy

xydyf−1(f(x))​=f−1(y)=f(x)=f′(x)dx=x​

Substitute:

∫f−1(y)dy=∫f−1(f(x))f′(x)dx=∫xf′(x)dx

Integrate by parts:

∫f−1(y)dy=∫xf′(x)dx=xf(x)−∫f(x)dx

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Integration

Tags: Math Calculus Integration Theory

What is the fundamental theorem of calculus (FTC)?

Consider a function f which is continuous on [a,b].

  • If F(x)=∫ax​(t)dt, then F′(x)=f(x) on (a,b).
  • If F is any antiderivative of f on [a,b] (i.e., F′(x)=f(x)), then ∫ab​f(x)dx=F(b)−F(a).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Integration

Tags: Math Calculus Integration Theory

dxd​(∫ab​f(t)dt)
dxd​​constantF(b)−F(a)​​​=0

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Integration

Tags: Math Calculus Integration Theory

dxd​(∫ax​f(t)dt)
dxd​​F(x)−constantF(a)​​​=f(x)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Integration

Tags: Math Calculus Integration Theory

dxd​(∫g(a)g(x)​f(t)dt)
dxd​​F(g(x))−constantF(g(a))​​​=f(g(x))dxdg(x)​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is an infinite sequence?

An infinite sequence is an ordered and indexed list of numbers of the form

a1​,a2​,a3​,…,an​,…

denoted as

{an​}n=1∞​

or simply

{an​}.

Each number an​ is a term of the sequence.

The subscript n is the index variable of the sequence.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the different ways to define a sequence?

An infinite sequence can be defined in various ways:

  • By listing terms {2,4,6,8,…}.
  • By an explicit formula that defines the n-th term directly in terms of n, an​=f(n).
  • By a recurrence relation, a rule that defines a term based on previous terms in the sequence, an​=f(an−1​,an−2​,…).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the limit of a sequence?

A sequence {an​} converges to a real number L if for all ϵ>0, there exists an integer N such that ∣an​−L∣<ϵ if n≥N. The number L is the limit of the sequence and we write

n→∞lim​an​=L or an​→L.

In this case, we say the sequence {an​} is a convergent sequence. If a sequence does not converge, it is a divergent sequence, and we say the limit does not exist.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the squeeze theorem for sequences?

Consider sequences {an​}, {bn​}, and {cn​}. Suppose there exists an integer N such that an​≤bn​≤cn​ for all n≥N. If there exists a real number L such that

n→∞lim​an​=L=n→∞lim​cn​,

then {bn​} converges and

n→∞lim​bn​=L

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is a bounded sequence?

A sequence {an​} is bounded above if there exists a real number M such that

an​≤M

for all positive integers n.

A sequence {an​} is bounded below if there exists a real number M such that

M≤an​

for all positive integers n.

A sequence {an​} is a bounded sequence if it is bounded above and bounded below.

If a sequence is not bounded, it is an unbounded sequence.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the relationship between convergent and bounded sequences?

If a sequence {an​} converges, then it is bounded.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is a monotone sequence?

A sequence {an​} is increasing for all n≥n0​ if

an​≤an+1​ for all n≥n0​.

A sequence {an​} is decreasing for all n≥n0​ if

an​≥an+1​ for all n≥n0​.

A sequence {an​} is a monotone sequence for all n≥n0​ if it is increasing for all n≥n0​ or decreasing for all n≥n0​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the monotone convergence theorem?

If {an​} is a bounded sequence and there exists a positive integer n0​ such that {an​} is monotone for all n≥n0​, then {an​} converges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is an infinite series?

An infinite series is a sum of the terms of an infinite sequnce an​:

n=1∑∞​an​=a1​+a2​+a3​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the harmonic series?

The harmonic series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​n1​

The harmonic series is a special case of the p-series with p=1.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the harmonic series?

The harmonic series ∑n=1∞​n1​ diverges to infinity.

Related:

The alternate harmonic series ∑n=1∞​(−1)n+1n1​ converges to ln2.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the alternating harmonic series?

The alternating harmonic series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1n1​=1−21​+31​−41​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the alternate harmonic series?

The alternate harmonic series ∑n=1∞​(−1)n+1n1​ converges to ln2.

Related:

The harmonic series ∑n=1∞​n1​ diverges to infinity.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the geometric series?

The geometric series is an infinite series of the following form

n=0∑∞​arn=a+ar+ar2+ar3+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the geometric series?

The geometric series ∑n=0∞​arn converges to 1−ra​ if ∣r∣<1, otherwise it diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the p-series?

The p-series series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​np1​=1p1​+2p1​+3p1​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the p-series?

The p-series ∑n=1∞​np1​ converges if p>1, otherwise it diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the telescoping series?

The telescoping series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​an​−an+1​.

In other words, the telescoping series is a series in which most of the terms cancel in each of the partial sums, leaving only some of the first terms and some of the last terms.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the algebraic properties of convergent series?

Let ∑n=1∞​an​ and ∑n=1∞​bn​ be convergent series. Then the following algebraic properties hold.

  • Sum Rule: The series ∑n=1∞​(an​+bn​) converges and ∑n=1∞​(an​+bn​)=∑n=1∞​an​+∑n=1∞​bn​.
  • Difference Rule: The series ∑n=1∞​(an​−bn​) converges and ∑n=1∞​(an​−bn​)=∑n=1∞​an​−∑n=1∞​bn​.
  • Constant Multiple Rule: For any real number c, the series ∑n=1∞​can​ converges and ∑n=1∞​can​=c∑n=1∞​an​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the divergence test for a series?

Consider the sequence an​. If limn→∞​an​=0 or limn→∞​an​ does not exist, then the series ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.

The converse is not true. That is, if limn→∞​an​=0, we cannot make any conclusion about the convergence of ∑n=1∞​an​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the integral test?

Suppose ∑n=0∞​an​ is a series with positive terms an​. Suppose there exists a function f and a positive integer N such that the following three conditions are satisfied:

  • f is continuous,
  • f is decreasing, and
  • f(n)=an​ for all integers n≥N.

Then ∑n=1∞​an​ and ∫N∞​f(x)dx both converge or both diverge.

Although convergence of the integral implies convergence of the related series, it does not imply that the value of the integral and the series are the same.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

How to estimate the value of a series?

TODO

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

How to estimate the remainder of a series?

TODO

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the direct comparison test?

Suppose there exists an integer N such that 0≤an​≤bn​ for all n≥N. If ∑n=1∞​bn​ converges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges.

Suppose there exists an integer N such that an​≥bn​≥0 for all n≥N. If ∑n=1∞​bn​ diverges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the limit comparison test?

Let an​, bn​≥0 for all n≥1.

If limn→∞​an​/bn​=L=0, then ∑n=1∞​an​ and ∑n=1∞​bn​ both converge or both diverge.

If limn→∞​an​/bn​=0 and ∑n=1∞​bn​ converges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges.

If limn→∞​an​/bn​=∞ and ∑n=1∞​bn​ diverges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the alternating series?

The alternating series is a sum of the terms of an infinite sequnce an​ whose terms alternate between positive and negative values:

n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1an​=a1​−a2​+a3​−…

or

n=1∑∞​(−1)nan​=−a1​+a2​−a3​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the alternating series test?

An alternating series of the form ∑n=1∞​(−1)n+1an​ or ∑n=1∞​(−1)nan​ converges if 0<an+1​≤an​ for all n≥1 and limn→∞​an​=0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the remainder of an alternating series?

TODO

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the relationship between absolute and conditional convergence?

A series ∑n=1∞​an​ exhibits absolute convergence if ∑n=1∞​∣an​∣ converges.

A series ∑n=1∞​an​ exhibits conditional convergence if ∑n=1∞​an​ converges but ∑n=1∞​∣an​∣ diverges.

If ∑n=1∞​∣an​∣ converges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the ratio test?

Consider the series ∑n=1∞​an​ with nonzero terms. Let

ρ=n→∞lim​​an​an+1​​​
  • If 0≤ρ<1, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges absolutely.
  • If ρ>1 or ρ=∞, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.
  • If ρ=1, the test does not provide any information.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the root test?

Consider the series ∑n=1∞​an​. Let

ρ=n→∞lim​n∣an​∣​
  • If 0≤ρ<1, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges absolutely.
  • If ρ>1 or ρ=∞, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.
  • If ρ=1, the test does not provide any information.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the power series?

The power series centered at x=0 is an infinite series of the following form

n=0∑∞​cn​xn=c0​+c1​x+c2​x2+…

The power series centered at x=a is an infinite series of the following form

n=0∑∞​cn​(x−a)n=c0​+c1​(x−a)+c2​(x−a)2+…

where x is a variable and the coefficients cn​ are constants.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the center of a power series?

The center of the power series ∑n=0∞​cn​(x−a)n is the point x=a, the value around which the series is expanded.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of a power series?

Consider the power series ∑n=0∞​cn​(x−a)n. The series satisfies exactly one of the following properties:

  • The series converges at x=a and diverges for all x=a.
  • The series converges for all real numbers x.
  • There exists a real number R>0 such that the series converges if ∣x−a∣<R and diverges if ∣x−a∣>R. At the values x where ∣x−a∣=R, the series may converge or diverge.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the interval of convergence of a power series?

The interval of convergence I of the power series ∑n=0∞​cn​(x−a)n is the set of all real numbers x such that the series converges. It's the "domain" of the power series.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Vector equation of the line.

r=p+td

where:

  • r is the position vector of any point on the line,
  • p is the position vector of a specific point on the line,
  • d is the direction vector of the line, and
  • t is a scalar parameter.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Parametric equation of the line.

xyz​=px​+tdx​=py​+tdy​=pz​+tdz​​

where:

  • (px​,py​,pz​) are the coordinates of the specific point on the line,
  • (dx​,dy​,dz​) are the components of the direction vector, and
  • t is a scalar parameter.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Symmetric equation of the line.

dx​x−px​​=dy​y−py​​=dz​z−pz​​

where:

  • (px​,py​,pz​) are the coordinates of the specific point on the line,
  • (dx​,dy​,dz​) are the components of the direction vector.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Vector equation of the plane.

(r−p)⋅n=0

or equivalently:

(r−p)⋅nr⋅n−p⋅nr⋅nr⋅n​=0=0=p⋅n=c​

where:

  • r is the position vector of any point on the plane,
  • p is the position vector of a specific point on the plane,
  • n is a normal vector to the plane, and
  • c=r⋅n is a scalar constant.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Sclar equation of the plane.

ax+by+cz=d

where:

  • (a,b,c) are the components of the normal vector n,
  • (x,y,z) are the coordinates of any point on the plane, and
  • d is a scalar constant.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Parametric equation of the plane.

r=p+sb+tc

where:

  • r is the position vector of any point on the plane,
  • p is the position vector of a specific point on the plane,
  • b is the first vector parallel to the plane,
  • c is the second vector parallel to the plane,
  • s and t are scalar parameters.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Whatis a Quadric Surface?

A Quadric Surface is a surface in three-dimensional space whose equation is a second-degree polynomial in the variables x, y and z.

The most general Quadric Surface has the form

​Ax2+By2+Cz2+Dxy+Eyz+Fxz+Gx+Hy+Iz+J=0​

where A, B, C,... are all constants.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Ellipsoid centered at the origin.

a2x2​+b2y2​+c2z2​=1

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Elliptic Paraboloid centered at the origin.

z=a2x2​+b2y2​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Hyperbolic Paraboloid centered at the origin.

z=a2x2​−b2y2​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Hyperboloid of One Sheet centered at the origin.

a2x2​+b2y2​−c2z2​=1

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Hyperboloid of Two Sheets centered at the origin.

a2x2​+b2y2​−c2z2​=−1

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Elliptic Cone centered at the origin.

c2z2​=a2x2​+b2y2​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What is the algebraic definition the Dot Product?

Consider two vectors a and b in an n-dimensional space a=⟨a1​,a2​,…,an​⟩, b=⟨b1​,b2​,…,bn​⟩.

The Dot Product of a⋅b is computed as the sum of the products of the corresponding components:

a⋅b=a1​b1​+a2​b2​+…+an​bn​=i=1∑n​ai​bi​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What is the geometric definition the Dot Product?

Consider two vectors a and b in an n-dimensional space a=⟨a1​,a2​,…,an​⟩, b=⟨b1​,b2​,…,bn​⟩.

The Dot Product of a⋅b is defined in terms of the magnitudes (lengths) of the vectors and the angle between them:

a⋅b=∥a∥∥b∥cosθ

where

  • ∥a∥ is the magnitude (length) of vector a,
  • ∥a∥ is the magnitude (length) of vector b, and
  • θ is the angle between the two vectors.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What is the algebraic definition the Cross Product?

Consider two vectors a and b in the 3-dimensional space a=⟨a1​,a2​,a3​⟩, b=⟨b1​,b2​,b3​⟩.

The Cross Product of a×b is the determinant

a×b=​ia1​b1​​ja2​b2​​ka3​b3​​​

which expands to

a×b=(a2​b3​−a3​b2​)i−(a1​b3​−a3​b1​)j+(a1​b2​−a2​b1​)k

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What is the geometric definition the Cross Product?

Consider two vectors a and b in the 3-dimensional space a=⟨a1​,a2​,a3​⟩, b=⟨b1​,b2​,b3​⟩.

The Cross Product a×b is a vector that:

  • is perpendicular to both a and b (so orthogonal to the plane containing them),
  • has magnitude equal to the area of the parallelogram spanned by a and b:∥a×b∥=∥a∥∥b∥sinθ where θ is the angle between a and b, and
  • has direction given by the right-hand rule.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Unit Tangent Vector T to a parameterized curve.

The Unit Tangent Vector is the normalized velocity vector.

T(t)=∥r′(t)∥r′(t)​

where:

  • r(t)=⟨x(t),y(t),z(t)⟩ is a vector-valued function, and
  • r′(t)=⟨x′(t),y′(t),z′(t)⟩ is the velocity vector.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Principal Normal Vector N to a parameterized curve.

The Principal Normal Vector to a curve C is a unit vector that

  • is perpendicular to the Unit Tangent Vector, and
  • points in the direction that the curve is bending.
N(t)=∥T′(t)∥T′(t)​

where:

  • T(t) is the Unit Tangeng Vector.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

Binormal Vector B to a parameterized curve.

B(t)=T(t)×N(t)

where:

  • T(t) is the Unit Tangeng Vector, and
  • N(t) is the Principal Normal Vector.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What is a Vector Valued Function (VVF)?

A Vector Valued Function (VVF)is a function where the input is a scalar and the output is a vector.

r:D⊆R→Rn r(t)=(f1​(t),f2​(t),…,fn​(t))

Examples:

r(t)=(cos(t),sin(t)) r(t)=(t,t2,t3)

Related

Function of Several Variables: r:D⊆Rn→R

Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables: F:D⊆Rm→Rn

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What is a Function of Several Variables (FSV)?

A Function of Several Variables (FSV) is a function where the input is a vector and the output is a scalar.

f:D⊆Rn→R f(x1​,x2​,…,xn​)

Examples:

f(x,y)=x2+y2 f(x)=∥x∥2

Related

Function of Several Variables: r:D⊆Rn→R

Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables: F:D⊆Rm→Rn

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What kind of a field does a Function of Several Variables define?

A Function of Several Variables r:D⊆Rn→R defines a Scalar Field.

Related

Function of Several Variables: r:D⊆Rn→R

Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables: F:D⊆Rm→Rn

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What is a Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables (VVFSV)?

A Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables (VVFSV)is a function where the input is a vector x∈Rm and the output is a vector y∈Rn.

F:D⊆Rm→Rn F(x)=(f1​(x),f2​(x),…,fn​(x)) x=(x1​,x2​,…,xm​)

Examples:

F(x,y)=(x2−y,x+y2) F(x,y,z)=(xy,yz,xz)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors

What kind of a field does a Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables define?

A Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables F:D⊆Rm→Rn defines a Vector Field.

Related

Function of Several Variables: r:D⊆Rn→R

Vector-Valued Function of Several Variables: F:D⊆Rm→Rn

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::ODE

Tags: Math Calculus Differential-Equation ODE Theory

What is an ordinary differential equation (ODE)?

An ordinary differential equation is a mathematical equation that relates an unknown function of a single independent variable to its derivatives with respect to that variable.

A general n-th order ordinary differential equation can be expressed as

F(x,y,y′,y′′,…,y(n))=0

where

  • x is the independent variable.
  • y is the unknown function of x, i.e., y=y(x).
  • y′,y′′,…,y(n) are the first, second, ..., n-th derivatives of y with respect to x.
  • F is a given function relating all these variables and derivatives.

The solution for a differential equation is a function which satisfies the above expression.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::ODE

Tags: Math Calculus Differential-Equation ODE Theory

What is the order of an ordinary differential equation?

The order of an ordinary differential equation is the order of the highest derivative in the equation.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::ODE

Tags: Math Calculus Differential-Equation ODE Theory

What is an autonomous ordinary differential equation?

An autonomous ordinary differential equation is an equation where the independent variable does not explicitly appear in the equation.

An autonomous ordinary differential equation can be expressed in the form:

dxdy​=f(y)

where f(y) is a function that depends only on the dependent variable y.

The equation can be solved by separating the variables and integrating both sides:

f(y)1​⋅dxdy​∫f(y)1​⋅dxdy​dx∫f(y)dy​​=1=∫1dx=x+C​

where C is the constant of integration.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::ODE

Tags: Math Calculus Differential-Equation ODE Theory

What is a separable ordinary differential equation?

A first-order ordinary differential equation is separable if it can be written in the form

dxdy​=h(x)g(y)

where h(x) is a function of x alone and g(y) is a function of y alone.

Once the variables are separated, we can integrate both sides of the equation with respect to x as follows:

∫g(y)1​⋅dxdy​dx∫g(y)dy​G(y)​=∫h(x)dx=∫h(x)dx=H(x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the definition of the Laplace transform?

The Laplace transform of a function f(t) is a function F(s) defined as

F(s)=L{f}=∫0∞​e−stf(t)dt

where f(t) is a function of exponential order defined for all t≥0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the first translation or shifting property of the Laplace transform?

If f(t) has the transform F(s) then

L{ectf(t)}=F(s−c) L−1{F(s−c)}=ectf(t)

Example:

Since

L{cos2t}=s2+4s​

we have

L{e3tcos2t}=F(s−3)=(s−3)2+4(s−3)​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the second translation or shifting property of the Laplace transform?

If f(t) has the transform F(s) then

L{f(t−c)u(t−c)}=e−csF(s) L−1{e−csF(s)}=f(t−c)u(t−c)

where u(t−c) is the unit step function, which is defined as

u(t−c)={10​t≥ct<c​

Example:

Since

L{t3}=s46​

we have

L{f(t−2)u(t−2)}=e−2sF(s)=e−2ss46​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the change of scale property of the Laplace transform?

If f(t) has the transform F(s) then

L{f(ct)}=c1​F(cs​) L−1{c1​F(cs​)}=f(ct)

Example:

Since

L{sint}=s2+11​

we have

L{sin3t}=31​F(3s​)=31​(s/3)2+11​=s2+93​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the unit step function u(t−c)?

The unit step function (Heaviside function) u(t−c) is defined as

u(t−c)={10​t≥ct<c​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the effect of multiplying by the unit step function u(t−c)?

Let f(t)=0 for all t<0.

  • f(t)u(t−c) with c>0 is f(t) switched on at the point c.
  • f(t−c)u(t−c) with c>0 is f(t) shifted (translated) to the right by the amount c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the unit impulse function δ(t−c)?

The unit impulse function (Dirac delta function) δ(t−c) is a function that has the following properties.

Infinite at the origin c, zero everywhere else:

δ(t−c)={∞0​t=ct=c​

Area of unity:

∫0∞​δ(t−c)dt=1

Shifting or sampling property:

∫−∞∞​f(t)δ(t−c)dt=f(c)

The unit impulse function is technically not a function but a generalized function or distribution.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the derivative of the unit step function?

The derivative of the unit step function (Heaviside function) is the unit impulse function (Dirac delta function);

dtd​u(t)=δ(t)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra

What is a linear equation?

A linear equation is an algebraic equation in which each term is either a constant or the product of a constant and a single variable. Linear equations can have one or more variables.

The general form of a linear equation in two variables x and y is:

ax+by=c

where a, b, and c are constants, and a and b are not both zero.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra

What is a system of linear equations?

A system of linear equations is a collection of one or more linear equations involving the same set of variables.

For example, the following is a system of two linear equations with two variables x and y:

2x+3y4x−y​=6=5​

The solution to a system of linear equations is the set of values for the variables that satisfy all the equations simultaneously.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra

What is a homogeneous system of linear equations?

A homogeneous system of linear equations is a system in which all of the constant terms are zero.

For example, the following is a homogeneous system of two linear equations with two variables x and y:

2x+3y4x−y​=0=0​

A homogeneous system always has at least one solution, known as the trivial solution, where all variables are equal to zero (e.g., x=0 and y=0).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra

What is the difference between a inconsistent and an consistent system of linear equations?

A system of linear equations is consistent if there is at least one set of values for the variables that satisfies all the equations simultaneously. In other words, a consistent system has at least one solution.

A system of linear equations is inconsistent if there are no solutions. This occurs when the equations represent parallel lines (in two dimensions) or planes (in three dimensions) that do not intersect.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra

What is the difference between a dependent and an independent system of linear equations?

A system of linear equations is independent if the equations are not multiples of each other, meaning they provide unique information about the variables. An independent system has a unique solution.

A system of linear equations is dependent if at least one equation can be derived from the others, meaning they do not provide unique information about the variables. A dependent system has either infinitely many solutions or no solution.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What is a diagonal matrix?

A diagonal matrix is a square matrix in which all entries outside the main diagonal are zero. The main diagonal runs from the top left to the bottom right of the matrix.

For example, the following is a 3×3 diagonal matrix:

​a11​00​0a22​0​00a33​​​

where a11​, a22​, and a33​ are the diagonal elements.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What is a upper-trianguar matrix?

An upper-triangular matrix is a square matrix in which all entries below the main diagonal are zero. The main diagonal runs from the top left to the bottom right of the matrix.

For example, the following is a 3×3 upper-triangular matrix:

​a11​00​a12​a22​0​a13​a23​a33​​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What is a lower-trianguar matrix?

An lower-triangular matrix is a square matrix in which all entries above the main diagonal are zero. The main diagonal runs from the top left to the bottom right of the matrix.

For example, the following is a 3×3 lower-triangular matrix:

​a11​a21​a31​​0a22​a32​​00a33​​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What is the identity matrix I?

The identity matrix I is a square matrix in which all the elements of the main diagonal are ones, and all other elements are zeros. The main diagonal runs from the top left to the bottom right of the matrix.

For example, the following is a 3×3 identity matrix:

​100​010​001​​

The identity matrix acts as the multiplicative identity in matrix multiplication, meaning that for any matrix A of compatible size:

AI=IA=A

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What is a symmetric matrix?

A symmetric matrix is a square matrix that is equal to its transpose. In other words, a matrix A is symmetric if:

A=AT

This means that the elements across the main diagonal are mirror images of each other, i.e., aij​=aji​ for all i and j.

For example, the following is a 3×3 symmetric matrix:

​123​245​356​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What are the multiplication properties of matrices?

Matrix multiplication follows the distributive law:

A(B±C)(A±B)C​=AB±AC=AC±BC​

Matrix multiplication follows the associative law:

A(BC)=(AB)C

In general, matrix multiplication is not commutative:

AB=BA

Except when multiplying by the identity matrix I:

AI=IA

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

Is matrix multiplication distributive?

Yes, matrix multiplication is distributive. For matrices A, B, and C (of compatible sizes), the following properties hold:

A(B±C)(A±B)C​=AB±AC=AC±BC​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

Is matrix multiplication associative?

Yes, matrix multiplication is associative. For matrices A, B, and C (of compatible sizes), the following properties hold:

A(BC)=(AB)C

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

Is matrix multiplication commutative?

No, matrix multiplication is not commutative. For any matrices A and B:

AB=BA

Except when multiplying by the identity matrix I:

AI=IA

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What is the inverse of a square matrix?

The inverse of a square matrix A is a square matrix A−1 such that:

A⋅A−1=A−1⋅A=I

where I is the identity matrix of the same size as A.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

What are the properties of symmetric matrices?

Suppose that is A symmetric matrix. Then, we have the following properties:

  • −A is symmetric.
  • cA is symmetric for any c=0.
  • Am is symmetric for any positive integer m.
  • A−1 is symmetric for nonsingular A.

If A and B symmetric matrices, we have the following properties:

  • The sum A+B is symmetric.
  • The product A⋅B is not always symmetric.

Finally:

  • A+AT is symmetric for any square matrix A.
  • A⋅AT and AT⋅A is symmetric for any m×n matrix A.

Any diagonal matrix is symmetric.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

If A is a symmetric matrix, is Am symmetric?:

Yes, Am is symmetric for a symmetric matrix A and a positive integer m.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra

Tags: Math Linear-Algebra Matrix

If A is a symmetric matrix, is A−1 symmetric?:

Yes, A−1 is symmetric for a nonsingular symmetric matrix A.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Linear Algebra
